speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two interviewing Ben Kamm, Studio City, California, USA. Ben, you stopped where you were trying to join the other partisan in the east.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We decided to go to the Polish Ukraine because we heard that more partisan groups are forming in the East from Poland. So it took us a long way to go from Lublin to Kowal [INAUDIBLE] eastern Poland. On the way, we had to fight the Germans, we had to fight the Ukrainian partisans who were anti-Jewish, anti-everything. And we came to the Ukraine and we met a big, big partisan group. The commander of this group was a hero of the Soviet Union, General Federowitz Federow. Federow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was part of the Russian army. The number of this group was 0015. It was part of Ukrainian front. We used to get supplies from Russia every single day. We used to drop mines, guns, all kinds of things to arm the people to fight against the German. But our main work was to mine the railroad tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Going from Germany to the Eastern Front. We occupied two railroad lines. So every single day, groups of 10, 12 used to go and damage the German trains. At first it was easier because if the locomotive hit the mine, the locomotive exploded, and the train was-- you couldn't do anything. But the Germans got smart. They used to put two flat cars in front of the locomotive which sent and when they hit the mine, only the flat car would destroy it. But also when you destroyed the train, some Germans were killed from the explosion. But we used to sit there on the line and watch what's going to happen, because we had to give a report what damage we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '43, our group destroyed 541 trains going to the Eastern front, killed 1,800 soldiers, 27 officers. How we know the amount? Because the Germans used to take carpenters from the neighborhood to build for them the coffins for burial. So we knew from the carpenters how many coffins they made. So we knew how many people got killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, also, they had Hungarians guarding the railroad tracks. Every about 100 yards was a bunker made out of cement. And one or two Hungarians used to sit there with a gun and watch the tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't feed them. The German didn't feed them. So they need food to eat. So they had a password give to the Polish people in the neighborhood. But the Polish people used to bring them food. So they knew the password. They used to give us the password. We used to know the password. We used to go there, and kill them in the bunker, and do our work on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, can I ask you a question? Did you know about any death camps, concentration camps, when you were in the partisan?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, our commander sent few Polish people because they saw trains loaded with people. They didn't know where they go. And this was the train going to Majdanek, Treblinka. And we found out in 1943 about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But never, never came-- look, we couldn't attack a camp like Majdanek or something like this. But in 1943 or '42, we were near Lublin. We heard about the little labor camp, Janiszow Lubelski. We used to drink a lot. A lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians all did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we heard about the camp. We decided a few Russian guys, a few Jewish guys-- we decided to go and hit the camp. But the problem was that the camp was so situated-- was in a plain field. I mean, was very hard to-- because you could be spotted during the day. At night, you don't know where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we found a hole in this like a little desert. We stayed there in this hole. We were eight guys. We stayed in the hole a whole day. And when the sun went down, we start to crawl to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already dark. We came to the entrance to the camp. There was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], I'll never forget, with a big beard. With a big beard. And two Ukrainians. They saw, they run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the camp and told the people that they are free. And we asked them, where's the commander? The commander was SS-- Volksdeutsche from Zakopane, Peter Ignor-- SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, I took away two of his cuff links from the shirt I had. And one of the Russians killed him. And had beautiful horses there. We let the horses go. We let the people go anyplace they wanted, but nobody went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't you take them in with you to the partisans? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took with me one man, Dr. Gross from Krakow. Dr. Gross was in this camp. And was another man in this camp who later on I met in Los Angeles. Henrik Nussbaum was liberated in this camp by us. But Dr. Gross had a love affair with a woman in Krakow. He had a boy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was with me for one year, and then he disappeared. He disappeared. I didn't know where or what. After the war I found out. Dr. Gross went to Krakow, then he wound up in Plaszow concentration camp. He and the camp commandant took part of the selections, who should live, who should die. He was later on with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler. He went with Schindler to Czechoslovakia, to the factory there. Then he came back to Poland after liberation, and the Polish government found out about his collaboration with the Nazis. And they made a trial and was killed by the Polish government.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean he was a kapo? He was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Nazis involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: About eight years ago, his son came to Los Angeles. He found out about me that I was with his father. He want to find out what kind of person his father was. He lived in Israel. So he spent with me five hours, recorded everything, and this is the last thing I heard about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to after you liberated that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the partisans. So we used to go on the railroad, and mine the railroad, blow up bridges. Used to go on the road, make ambushes on trucks and cars. Matter of fact, I have a piece of a grenade up in my nose. We went-- we heard in a little village there were 16 Germans there living in a big landowner's house. So we thought, let's go and get them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went there. And while I was opening the door on the bottom, the guy, the German on top, threw a grenade at us. And I just heard the noise. Ssh. The explosion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I fell and I was saved. So we got all the Germans, 16 Germans. We killed them and threw them in the river, in the lake there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other guys were safe, too, that went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The other guys were safe. So I still have a piece in my nose and a piece in my arm. Then we-- that's what we did all the time. We used to go-- we had big, big fights with the Ukrainian partisans. Matter of fact, this was on the Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Russian partisans that fight with the Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians were fascists. They're fascists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the fascists. The [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: The Vlasov. The Vlasov.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Vlasov. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vlasov army. Then in 1940-- end of '43 or beginning of '44, an order came [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to organize Polish citizens from the big group we were there, Russian group, to organize a Polish group. They gave a name, [NON-ENGLISH]. We were 1,200 men. We had to go back to Poland and our main work was to arm the Polish population, give everybody guns to fight the Germans. That's what we did. But you have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me for interrupting. Did the Polish population want to fight against the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were not only against the Jews. They were against the Ukrainians. They were against the Germans, too. They were against everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came back. We were walking long, long time. Because we had-- the AK used to make ambush on us. The Ukrainians made ambush on us. It was very, very hard. Many times I did not know I was going to survive the next hour. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from the Russian group, at that time-- Now wait a moment. You felt much safer when you were with the Russian group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, much safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Much safer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Much safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when you were separated you didn't feel safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had a lot-- a few from the group we took-- there were other Polish Jews who were in the big group came with us back to Poland. So we-- every day used to get drops by airplane from Russia. We buried thousands and thousands of guns in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what we did. We didn't fight the Germans. Germans didn't bother us. Because the Germans were afraid. The Germans were afraid to come to the forest. The Germans didn't come into the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, the Germans send 75,000 troops to get rid of our partisan group. And they encircled us in a quarter-mile square in the forest, and we had the biggest fight in history. As a matter of fact, it's written in the Polish history books. We fought the Germans for 16 hours. I saw the Germans, I would say, 10 yard away. We look each other's eyes, so nearby. Then we attacked the German line and we broke through the German lines. We got out of the encirclement. And went our way. Next day, a group of Armenian who served in the German army attacked us. We fought with them. We just split the whole group and just run away. And every day we had fights with the AK, with all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then near the front we heard stories that the front was nearing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we-- yes. Also we established a camp with our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest, we established a camp to help Jews who were hiding in the forest. We took them with us. And we had 3,000 old, men, and women, and children put on the Russian side to be saved. We took them from the forest. They were hiding in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean people that run away from their towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from their towns. They were hiding in the forest. Was a gruesome thing what I saw. Was gruesome things, what they did. The Germans said that the Polish did, the Polish did, that the Germans did. 40 Jewish women killed in the forest. The most gruesome-- all shot in the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean the Germans said the Germans did and the Polish? Did you catch anybody at that time that did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I say we caught a lot of Germans. A lot. We didn't take any prisoners. We interrogated them and that's it, the end of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Got rid of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody had-- I didn't do nothing. I didn't do anything. I have a wife and children, you know. This is-- everybody-- nobody was guilty. Then 1944, we came-- yeah, the front was nearing. We heard already the artillery [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knocking and all kinds of things. One day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was walking-- oh, yeah. One day, I was sent with another guy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on lookout to see what's in a little village, what's going on there. So we came to the village and the Germans saw us. So they straight on machine gun. Then we went from another side. They saw us again, they straight with a machine gun. And, you know, the toilets on the villages-- they didn't have toilets. They have a hole behind a house, and a little lumber. And that's how they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Outhouses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outhouses. And I was going back with my friend. And we fill in one of the holes. And we stayed there for four hours. Next thing the morning, you got out of it. Well, you don't have nothing to wear. So you had to go to a house someplace and you take away clothing from a farmer to get to dress, to put on. First of all, I didn't sleep in a bed for 3 years. I slept on snow, on ice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never bathed, never washed. I had millions of lice. I had a sickness, a skin sickness, that my clothing was stuck to my body. To take off my clothing means take off my skin. But somebody knew about horses. He said, horses have some sickness like skin sickness. So they give them certain things. So we got from a farmer a green, like a paint.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sulfur?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Paint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a paint. And they painted my every place and that's how I was cured. I wasn't cured because I came to America still. My cousin was a doctor in New Jersey who cured me with X-rays. Then we met. I went down--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can't understand. Being a partisan and being that you fought and everything, you didn't have the opportunity to go into a village and clean yourself up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you kidding?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm just asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you go to a village? The Germans, the Polish police, they can kill you. You cannot do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept on killing and running.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, even I had-- we used to make food for ourselves. Used to cook in a pail. I had a spoon. I had to hide the spoon in my boot because they shouldn't steal it. I had-- when I went to sleep, I used sleep with a gun in in my hand. My hands like this with the gun or to hold it nobody should steal it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even the partisan, they stole one from another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you never know. You never know who.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You never know. Because I used to walk around with a machine gun, 3,000 rounds of ammunition, four, five grenades, food. You know, and we walked every day 20, 30 kilometers. It's a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you followed a trail?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. A trail, one after the other. And the last one usually got shot. The last one got shot by the AK, by the Ukrainians. So we lost a lot of people. But in this big fight we had with the Germans, we lost 35 people. Nice, beautiful, beautiful people. Matter of fact, I had to remind myself. One guy committed suicide because when he saw the Germans, he committed. He was scared and shot himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a Jew or a Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Russian. A Russian. And I had a very dear, very close friend, a Russian-- Nikolai from Odessa-- who also committed suicide. So that was going on. But later on we had a doctor and a doctor. We had a hospital. We had already better, better. If somebody was wounded, I mean, they couldn't help it, was transported. The plane landed and used to took them to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Russian army came in Poland, we were near Chelm. And one day, one morning, I was going out with the-- to see what's happening. We met the Russian forward troops. And they start. They were, who you are and all this. They were afraid, you know? That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we told them we are the partisans and I spoke Russian. And, all right. So they were fighting for Janow Lubelski, a town. So we mined the roads there. The Germans couldn't run away. And took Janow Lubelski. Then we went into Chelm. Chelm was liberated already. And we established our group. Our partisan group, [NON-ENGLISH] became the police. The first Polish police after the war. The first Polish liberated city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Chelm was that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chelm was the city. And I had very close friends who were with me during the war in the partisans. After few weeks, one of my friends was killed in the street. Few weeks later, another friend of mine was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chelm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chelm in 1944. Then, anyway, I lost four friends after the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation. We buried them in the main street of the city, Ulica Lubelska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be Ukrainian or the AK. One day, I walked out of the movie and they were shooting after me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to go to Lublin every week. So I asked my commander officer that he should transfer me. I said, look what's happened. They're going to kill me here. So he said, no, I cannot do it. You have to stay here. Were there Jews in Chelm after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few. A few. They were liberated. 16 Jews were liberated in town and they were in jail. The Germans put them in jail. And then the Germans couldn't have time to do anything with them. So they survived. Because the Germans run away in pajamas there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you asked them to be transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] said no. So I took my gun in my pocket. And I had another friend who was born in Chelm, Juzek Zirsek. And I ran away. I went to Danzig. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had no other choice. I want to live. Poland was not such a good country to me to lose my life for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you gave your life for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't want to lose your life to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Because after the war, the anti-Semitism was unbelievable. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, how did you find out about your family? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out. I went to different communities. You see, in Poland, every city had a community. They had-- where they had a register. So who survived left a note. I survived, so and so, so and so, so and so. And that's how it is. When I came to Danzig, a few days later I went met Elly And I met a Polish, a ship--

speaker: interviewer
text: Elly, your wife now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife now. I met a Polish-- Polish, I said. An American boy in Danzig who came with a boat from America to bring cows for-- my God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: To have more cows. How you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: To breed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To breed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Polish government. And I met him. He didn't speak--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. English. Somehow, I said to him, I have an aunt in New York in Brooklyn. I have an aunt. I give you address. Could you go there, tell her that I'm alive? This was my father's sister. My father had two sisters here. And I gave him the piece of paper. I still remember the address. 2301 Coney Island Avenue, in Brooklyn. He went there. He met my aunt. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the mail start coming and I got a package from them-- is it? Or a letter or something. I got a package from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I met a French officer. My father had a family in France and had a brother there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that already? '45?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father had a--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a brother, and I remembered the address in Paris. So I asked the French officer who spoke German. I spoke to him. So I said to him, you know, I had an uncle, my father's brother in Paris. This and this address. But I-- there was no mail. Would you be so kind. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Find him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Find him and tell him that I survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he went to him. He went to my uncle and talked to him. And then somehow the mail was starting to come. And my uncle sent me a little package from France, from Paris. And I got it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my uncle told me his story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on now. Going to change tapes.
